content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 12, 1976

MR. James S. Kostas
City Attorney
1305 E. Palmdale Boulevard
Post Office Box 430
Palmdale, CA. 93550

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-046

Dear Mr. Kostas:

Thank you for your letter dated March 30, 1976, requesting
the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an opinion
concerning possible conflicts of interest of several city
councilmen. The Commission's regulations concerning requests
for opinions require that a representative disclose the
identity of the person for whom he is seeking an opinion. 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18320(b). Because your opinion request fails to do this, no formal opinion will be issued.
However, I hope the following informal comments will be
helpful to you in dealing with the situation you present.

Your questions are phrased in rather general terms.
For instance, you have not provided specific facts concerning
the nature or the extent of the impact of the decisions
which will affect Great Western Cities, Inc. (GWC), nor have
you indicated the type of employment of the spouses of the
councilmen. Accordingly, specific responses to your hypotheticals cannot be given. However, I have enclosed a copy
of the Commission's regulations in the Conflict of Interest
area, and, in particular, I would direct your attention to 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 concerning "Material Financial
Effect." With this regulation and the others I have enclosed
as a frame of reference, I can comment on each of your four
questions.

With respect to question one, the Political Reform Act
does not preclude the city (or any governmental body) from
entering into contracts with any entity. Rather, it proscribes participation by public officials with financial
interests in the decision to contract. (See, Gov. Code
Sections 87100 and 87103.) In addition, you should be aware
of the implications of Gov. Code Section 1090, at seq.,
relative to this question.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5501 · ENGAGEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Mr. James S. Kostas
April 12, 1976
Page Two


As to question two, it is possible that income of a
spouse may be a disclosable item and a source of disquali-
fication in certain circumstances. Section 87030 defines
"income" as "including any community property interest in
income of a spouse." However, as mentioned above, the reg-
ulations concerning "Material Financial Effect" are relevant
to the question of possible disqualification.

As to question three, neither Councilman C or D appears,
by virtue of the facts recited, to have a "Financial interest"
in G&C within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. See
Gov. Code Sections 87103, 82033 and 82030.

Finally, as to question four, I can perceive no dif-
ference, in terms of the requirements of the Political Reform
Act, between Councilman A's participation as a councilman
and his participation as mayor. If an official's financial
interest may be materially affected by a decision, the public
official may not "make, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his official position to influence ..." the
decision. The Commission's regulations, Section 18700(b)-
(f), interpret these provisions to proscribe the public
official from taking virtually any kind of action concerning
the decision.

I hope the enclosed regulations and these comments are
instructive. If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion
request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18321.

If you have any further questions, please contact Kenneth
Goshorn of the Conflict of Interest Division at (916) 322-
6441. Thank you for writing and for your interest in the
Political Reform Act.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman for the
Commission

DHL:oyo
Enclosure

KOSTAS & SPANN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

JAMES E. KOSTAS RICHARD I. SPANN
2925 PALOWDALE DRIVE WEST LOS ANGELES
FIRST OFFICE BOX 482 2670 W. 27TH STREET
PALODALE, CALIFORNIA 93510 LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90016
(805) 947-2622 (213) 542-2622

March 30, 1976

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

The undersigned is the City Attorney of the City of California
City, located in Kern County. At the municipal election earlier
this month three new councilmen were elected to office. Certain
questions have arisen since then regarding possible conflicts of
interest by some of these councilmen or by other incumbent
councilmen. In view of this I have been requested to ask an
opinion of your office. Under the circumstances, the City would
very much appreciate it if these questions could be resolved as
quickly as possible.

FACTS: California City is the largest city, areawise, in the State
of California. Great Western Cities, Inc., a corporation (here-
after referred to as GWC) is the largest owner of real property in
the City (probably in the area of 80%. In the past the City has
entered into a number of contracts and agreements with GWC, and
at the present time is involved in litigation against GWC. In
addition, it is contemplated that in the future there will be other
agreements or dealings between the City and GWC.

Councilmen A and B have spouses who are employed directly by GWC (or
a wholly owned subsidiary of GWC), and have been so employed for
more than a year. Councilman C owns a mobile home which is financed
directly by GWC, i.e., GWC holds a note and first deed of trust
on the mobile home as security for the balance of the purchase price.
Councilman D owns a residence which is also financed in the same
manner by a note and first deed of trust in favor of GWC. Neither
of these four councilmen is directly or indirectly employed by GWC
or any of its subsidiaries, nor are they officers, directors,
trustees, or holders of any management positions in GWC, nor do
they have any direct or indirect investment in GWC or any of its
subsidiaries.

QUESTIONS: 1. Does either Councilman A, B, C or D have a direct
conflict of interest to the extent that the City would be pre-
cluded from entering into any contracts or other dealings with GNC,
either under Proposition 9 or under Section 1090, et seq. of the
Government Code?

2. Do Councilmen A and B have a conflict of interest, due to the
employment of their spouses, which would require them to make dis-
closure and to refrain from voting on either contracts or other
dealings with GNC?

3. Does either Councilman C or D have a remote conflict of interest
with respect to GNC that would require them to disclose such fact,
and to refrain from voting on any contracts or other matters affect-
ing GNC?

4. The person referred to above as Councilman A is also the Mayor
of the City. In the event he is felt to have a remote conflict
of interest, would it be necessary for him to relinquish the chair
when the council is considering contracts or other matters relating
to GNC?

Your early reply to the above questions would be greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

JAMES S. KOSTAS, City Attorney
City of California City

JSKdn

cc: Mayor Donald Conley
    Dick Isenberg


-2-

sections.question:
1. Does either Councilman A, B, C or D have a direct conflict of interest to the extent that the City would be precluded from entering into any contracts or other dealings with GWC, either under Proposition 9 or under Section 1090, et seq. of the Government Code?

2. Do Councilmen A and B have a conflict of interest, due to the employment of their spouses, which would require them to make disclosure and to refrain from voting on either contracts or other dealings with GWC?

3. Does either Councilman C or D have a remote conflict of interest with respect to GWC that would require them to disclose such fact, and to refrain from voting on any contracts or other matters affecting GWC?

4. The person referred to above as Councilman A is also the Mayor of the City. In the event he is felt to have a remote conflict of interest, would it be necessary for him to relinquish the chair when the council is considering contracts or other matters relating to GWC?

You early reply to the above questions would be greatly appreciated.
sections.facts:
California City is the largest city, areawise, in the State of California. Great Western Cities, Inc., a corporation (hereafter referred to as GWC) is the largest owner of real property in the City (probably in the area of 80%). In the past the City has entered into a number of contracts and agreements with GWC, and at the present time is involved in litigation against GWC. In addition, it is contemplated that in the future there will be other agreements or dealings between the City and GWC.

Councilmen A and B have spouses who are employed directly by GWC (or a wholly owned subsidiary of GWC), and have been so employed for more than a year. Councilman C owns a mobile home which is financed directly by GWC, i.e., GWC holds a note and first deed of trust on the mobile home as security for the balance of the purchase price. Councilman D owns a residence which is also financed in the same manner by a note and first deed of trust in favor of GWC. Neither of these four councilmen is directly or indirectly employed by GWC or any of its subsidiaries, nor are they officers, directors, trustees, or holders of any management positions in GWC, nor do they have any direct or indirect investment in GWC or any of its subsidiaries.
